request_debug.prompt_preview:
You rerank dead-startup candidates for a slopmortem pipeline. Given a fresh pitch and K candidates retrieved by hybrid search, score each candidate from four perspectives, then return ONLY the top 5 ranked from most to least relevant.

Perspectives (each scored 0-10 with a one-sentence rationale):
- business_model: how similar are the revenue, pricing, and customer-relationship shapes?
- market: how similar are the target buyers, market size, and demand drivers?
- gtm: how similar are the go-to-

response.text: {"ranked":[{"candidate_id":"quibi::media_content","perspective_scores":{"business_model":{"score":3,"rationale":"Quibi was a subscription-based streaming platform, not a free-to-play cosmetics/battle-pass model, though both are B2C digital content products targeting consumers on mobile."},"market":{"score":4,"rationale":"Both target broad global consumer audiences on mobile devices, but Quibi focused on passive video viewers while the pitch targets active 4X strategy gamers."},"gtm":{"score":3,"rationale":"Both relied on app-store distribution and consumer marketing, but Quibi leaned heavily on premium content partnerships rather than game community/streamer GTM motions."},"stage_scale":{"score":4,"rationale":"Quibi was a well-funded, high-profile consumer mobile launch at scale with massive backing, which somewhat mirrors a global multi-platform game launch, though the contexts differ significantly."}},"rationale":"Quibi is the closest match as a B2C mobile-first digital entertainment product that failed at launch due to product-market fit issues, but its subscription video model and non-gaming content make it only a partial analog to a free-to-play 4X strategy game."},{"candidate_id":"blockbuster::retail_ecommerce","perspective_scores":{"business_model":{"score":1,"rationale":"Blockbuster was a physical/digital video rental and later subscription business with no resemblance to free-to-play cosmetics or battle-pass monetization."},"market":{"score":2,"rationale":"Both serve broad consumer entertainment markets, but Blockbuster's market was passive video rental/streaming consumers, not mobile/PC gamers."},"gtm":{"score":1,"rationale":"Blockbuster relied on physical retail stores and later DVD-by-mail, completely unlike app-store and Steam distribution for a F2P game."},"stage_scale":{"score":1,"rationale":"Blockbuster was a massive mature retail chain at a fundamentally different scale and lifecycle stage than a gaming studio shipping a new title."}},"rationale":"Blockbuster shares only the broad consumer entertainment sector; its business model, market, GTM, and scale are entirely dissimilar to a F2P mobile/desktop 4X strategy game."},{"candidate_id":"wikipedia.org","perspective_scores":{"business_model":{"score":2,"rationale":"Pebble sold hardware devices at a one-time price point, which is fundamentally different from free-to-play cosmetics and battle-pass monetization."},"market":{"score":2,"rationale":"Pebble targeted iOS and Android consumers globally, but its buyers were wearable tech enthusiasts rather than strategy gamers."},"gtm":{"score":3,"rationale":"Pebble used Kickstarter and app-ecosystem distribution across iOS and Android, which has some parallel to multi-platform app-store distribution for a game."},"stage_scale":{"score":3,"rationale":"Pebble was an indie/startup-scale consumer hardware company that shipped multiple products before insolvency, loosely comparable to an indie studio scale."}},"rationale":"Pebble is a cross-platform iOS/Android consumer product startup, but its hardware/one-time-purchase model and wearables market make it a weak analog to a F2P 4X strategy game studio."}]}
